title: Space Tech Briefing — June 13, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space cadets and Earth-bound explorers! Welcome to your daily dose of the cosmos. It's Friday, June 13, 2026, and we've got some fascinating updates from beyond our blue marble.

First up, fresh from the International Space Station, we're diving into the mysteries of botany! Our very own ESA astronaut, Sophie Adenot, has been conducting the groundbreaking ChlorISS experiment. She's been busy cultivating plants aboard the ISS, and the big question on everyone's mind – and hers – is: Do plants truly grow the same way in the microgravity environment of space as they do right here on Earth? This crucial research, led by CNES, is vital for understanding long-duration space missions and future extraterrestrial habitats. Stay tuned as we await the full findings!

And speaking of major developments, as we wait for today's new launches, let's look back at a major recent development that continues to ripple through the space community. Just a few days ago, on June 9th, NASA announced the crew for the monumental Artemis III mission! This is a huge step towards humanity's return to the Moon. We're thrilled to confirm that Italy's own ESA astronaut, Luca Parmitano, will be joining three American colleagues aboard the Orion spacecraft. This intermediate mission is set for an approximately two-week flight in low Earth orbit, where the crew will rigorously test the crucial docking procedures with the future Human Landing System. Remember, the ultimate goal is Artemis IV, targeting a lunar landing in 2028. Artemis III is a critical stepping stone, and Luca Parmitano's selection is a testament to international collaboration in space exploration!

What an incredible time to be alive, witnessing these leaps in space exploration and discovery! That's all for your morning space briefing. Thank you for tuning in, keep looking up, and have a fantastic day!
